brief_document.title: OPFS WAL backend with capability detection and IndexedDB fallback
brief_document.purpose: Deliver the OPFS-backed WAL for browsers that support it, with transparent fallback to IndexedDB when OPFS is unavailable. OPFS provides synchronous file access in web workers — a better fit than IndexedDB for the WAL's append-only pattern. The WAL entry format is byte-identical to native for portability.
brief_document.task: Implement OPFS WAL backend using synchronous file access in web worker contexts. Use the same WAL entry format and CRC32 checksums as the native durable WAL (PERSIST-002). Implement runtime capability detection for OPFS availability. When OPFS is unavailable, fall back to IndexedDB-backed WAL persistence. The fallback must be transparent to the shard actor — the WAL interface is identical regardless of backend.

requirements.title: OPFS WAL writer
requirements.spec: THE SYSTEM SHALL implement an OPFS-backed WAL writer that uses synchronous file access in web worker contexts for append-only writes. THE SYSTEM SHALL use the same WAL interface as the native DurableWal.
acceptance: OpfsWal implements the same WAL interface as DurableWal
OpfsWal uses synchronous file access via OPFS in web workers
Append operations write to the OPFS file sequentially

requirements.title: Native-compatible WAL entry format
requirements.spec: THE SYSTEM SHALL use the same WAL entry format (operation type, key, value, CRC32 checksum) in the OPFS backend as in the native durable WAL (PERSIST-002). THE SYSTEM SHALL produce byte-identical entries so that WAL files are portable between native and WASM builds.
acceptance: A WAL entry written by OPFS backend deserialises correctly by the native WAL reader
A WAL entry written by the native WAL writer deserialises correctly by the OPFS backend
CRC32 checksums are computed identically in both backends

requirements.title: Runtime OPFS capability detection
requirements.spec: THE SYSTEM SHALL detect OPFS availability at runtime. THE SYSTEM SHALL check for web worker context and OPFS API presence. THE SYSTEM SHALL return a capability enum (OpfsAvailable, OpfsUnavailable).
acceptance: detect_opfs() returns OpfsAvailable in a web worker with OPFS support
detect_opfs() returns OpfsUnavailable on the main thread
detect_opfs() returns OpfsUnavailable in browsers without OPFS

requirements.title: IndexedDB WAL fallback
requirements.spec: WHEN OPFS is unavailable, THE SYSTEM SHALL fall back to IndexedDB-backed WAL persistence. THE SYSTEM SHALL make the fallback transparent to the shard actor — the WAL interface is the same regardless of backend. THE SYSTEM SHALL document the durability limitation: IndexedDB transactions are not guaranteed to flush before page unload.
acceptance: When OPFS is unavailable, the WAL uses IndexedDB without error
The shard actor does not know which WAL backend it uses
The WAL interface (append, truncate, read) is identical for both backends
The durability limitation of IndexedDB-only mode is documented

boundaries: SHALL NOT implement the IndexedDB NodeStore — that is WASM-001
SHALL NOT implement browser transport — that is WASM-003
SHALL NOT implement a WAL format different from native — byte-identical is required
SHALL NOT implement OPFS on the main thread — synchronous access is only available in web workers
verification: cargo check -p haematite --target wasm32-unknown-unknown --features wasm succeeds
cargo test -p haematite --lib passes all tests (native)
cargo clippy -p haematite produces no errors
No file in crates/haematite/src/ exceeds 500 lines

adrs.title: zstd compression per content-addressed node, WAL uncompressed
adrs.decision: zstd compression per content-addressed tree node, WAL entries uncompressed, over compressing both or neither. The rejected alternatives: compressing WAL entries (poor ratio, added write latency on the hot path); no compression (wasted disk for tree nodes that compress well at ~3:1 for structured data).

checklist.text: OPFS WAL uses synchronous file access in web worker contexts for append-only writes.
OPFS WAL entry format is byte-identical to the native durable WAL format (CN6).
OPFS WAL includes CRC32 checksums per entry, matching the native format.
Runtime capability detection determines OPFS availability.
When OPFS is unavailable, the WAL falls back to IndexedDB-backed persistence (CN5).
Fallback is transparent to the shard actor — the WAL interface is the same regardless of backend.

stories.text: As an application developer, I want the WAL to use OPFS when available and fall back to IndexedDB when it is not so that durability is best-effort without configuration.
As an operations engineer, I want the WASM build to gracefully degrade when browser APIs (OPFS, web workers) are unavailable so that the database is functional in constrained environments.

constraints.text: WASM build must compile to wasm32-unknown-unknown without any native-only dependencies. All platform-specific code must be behind cfg gates.
BLAKE3 hashes must be identical between native and WASM builds. The portable BLAKE3 implementation must produce the same output as the SIMD-accelerated one.
WASM binary size (gzipped) must stay under 2MB. Dependencies that bloat the binary must be cfg-gated or replaced.
IndexedDB operations are async. The NodeStore adapter must not block the browser's main thread.
OPFS fallback to IndexedDB must be transparent to the shard actor. The WAL interface is the same regardless of backend.
The WAL entry format in OPFS must be byte-identical to the native durable WAL format so that WAL files are portable.

resolved_context.intention: The WASM cluster extends haematite to the browser and edge. When this cluster is complete, the same haematite API — get, put, range, fork, merge, commit — runs in a browser tab or Cloudflare Worker, backed by IndexedDB or OPFS instead of the filesystem. Content-addressed nodes are portable across backends: a tree written on a native server can be synced to a browser tab and read there with identical hashes. The experience should feel like the same database everywhere — write on the server, read in the browser, fork on the edge — not a separate product with a compatibility layer.
